raw_output:
Classification returned by Reducto: financial_statement  

Why that label makes sense:

• “Statement” language throughout – The first page banner reads “Retirement Account Statement” and later pages have section titles such as “Account Summary,” “Performance Summary,” “Asset Allocation,” “Portfolio Detail,” and “Transaction Detail.” These are canonical sections of a brokerage or bank statement.  
• Period covered – The document specifies a “Statement period” (e.g., “02/01/2023-02/28/2023”), which is typical of monthly or quarterly financial statements.  
• Account-level identifiers – It lists an account number, account registration, and the brokerage firm’s name/logo (“Fidelity Investments”), which are normal elements of statements issued by financial institutions.  
• Balances & performance metrics – Tables show beginning balance, deposits/withdrawals, market change, ending balance, security positions, cost basis, unrealized gain/loss, and rate-of-return percentages—precisely the data a financial statement delivers.  
• Regulatory disclosures – Fine-print footnotes reference SIPC coverage, performance calculation methodology, and investment risks—required disclaimers that appear on official account statements, not on invoices, contracts, or blank forms.  

Why the other categories were not selected:

• Invoice – No “Invoice #,” “Bill To,” line-item charges, taxes, payment due date, or total amount due.  
• Contract – Lacks clauses, signature blocks, effective dates, or governing-law language.  
• Form – There are no empty fields or check-boxes for a customer to complete.  
• Other – The document squarely matches the well-defined pattern of a brokerage account statement, so the more specific “financial_statement” category supersedes a generic “other.”
